coredata.dc:title: Single atomically anchored iron on graphene quantum dots for a highly efficient oxygen evolution reaction
coredata.dc:creator: Chang, C.-Y.
Kashale, A.A.
Lee, C.-M.
Chu, S.-L.
Lin, Y.-F.
Chen, I-W.P.
coredata.dc:description: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) supported on carbon-based materials are very promising for maximizing their electrocatalytic activity. However, carbon-based SACs are primarily bonded with carbons, resulting in an inferior performance in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Herein, we develop a novel coordination adsorption strategy for the synthesis of a monodispersed Fe(NO)2 moiety anchored on the nitrogen sites of the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to form a Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex as an efficient OER catalyst. The resultant Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex exhibits a highly stable overpotential of 270 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slope of 48 mV dec−1 together with long-term durability, which greatly outperforms the state-of-the-art RuO2. Our finding emphasizes the role of electron-transfer resistance changes during a simple synthesis method to enhance electrocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, this work will envision numerous opportunities for creating novel-type carbon-based SACs via nitrogen–metal coordination as highly robust OER catalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Dinitrosyl iron complex
Oxygen evolution reaction

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Taiwan Ministry of Science and Technology

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedures of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs.

(a) FT-IR spectra of Fe(CO)2(NO)2 (black line), N-doped GQDs-Fe(NO)2 (red line), PyBr3-Fe(CO)2(NO)2 (green line) and PEI-Fe(CO)2(NO)2 (dark brown). (b) Raman spectra of graphene, N-GQDs, and N-doped GQDs-Fe(NO)2. (c) UV–visible spectra of N-GQDs and N-doped GQDs-Fe(NO)2.
(a) TEM image of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. (b) Elemental mapping of iron in the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex. (c) EDS of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs.

Characterizations of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs: (a) aberration-corrected high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-STEM) image and the corresponding EDS mappings of (b) Fe and (c) N.
High-resolution XPS spectra of the (a) C 1s, (b) N 1s, and (c) Fe 2p peaks of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex.

(a) LSV curves of N-GQDs, Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, and RuO2 at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1 in a 0.1 M KOH electrolyte. (b) Tafel plot of N-GQDs, Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, and RuO2. (c) EIS spectra of N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. (d) Emission lifetime decay of N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs.

ECSA of (a) N-GQDs, (b) 0.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, (c) 1.0-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, (d) 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, and (e) 2.0-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs at different scan rates. (f) Parallel specific capacitance of N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs.
(a) Stability of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs at an applied potential of 1.52 V. (b) LSV curves of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs after 1 and 2,000 cycles under the same OER operation condition.
Comparative average value of τem at a given λex of N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs.

head.ce:title: Single atomically anchored iron on graphene quantum dots for a highly efficient oxygen evolution reaction

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, 369, Sec. 2, University Rd., Taitung City 95092, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Applied Science, National Taitung University, 369, Sec. 2, University Rd., Taitung City 95092, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, No. 1, Sec. 4, Roosevelt Rd., Taipei 10617, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, National Taiwan University, No. 1, Sec. 4, Roosevelt Rd., Taipei 10617, Taiwan

ce:footnote.ce:label: c
ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Single-atom catalysts (SACs) supported on carbon-based materials are very promising for maximizing their electrocatalytic activity. However, carbon-based SACs are primarily bonded with carbons, resulting in an inferior performance in the oxygen evolution reaction (OER). Herein, we develop a novel coordination adsorption strategy for the synthesis of a monodispersed Fe(NO)2 moiety anchored on the nitrogen sites of the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to form a Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex as an efficient OER catalyst. The resultant Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex exhibits a highly stable overpotential of 270 mV at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slope of 48 mV dec−1 together with long-term durability, which greatly outperforms the state-of-the-art RuO2. Our finding emphasizes the role of electron-transfer resistance changes during a simple synthesis method to enhance electrocatalytic efficiency. Therefore, this work will envision numerous opportunities for creating novel-type carbon-based SACs via nitrogen–metal coordination as highly robust OER catalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Renewable energy conversion and storage via electrochemical technologies are promising in addressing greenhouse gas emissions and severe global climate change [1–8]. The oxygen evolution reaction (OER) is the half-reaction of electrochemical water-splitting, which can be utilized to produce H2 using renewable energy [9]. The energy conversion efficiency of H2 production by water-splitting is usually hindered by the large overpotential of the kinetically sluggish OER. Therefore, noble metal oxide (IrO2 and RuO2) electrocatalysts have been widely used for OER electrocatalysis [10,11]. Unfortunately, the high price and rarity of these precious metal oxide electrocatalysts have restricted renewable electrochemical energy technologies toward commercialization. Motivated by this challenge, to reduce the use of (or even replace) precious-metal-based electrocatalysts, significant efforts have been dedicated to developing earth-abundant, non-precious-metal oxides, metal hydro(oxy)oxides, and their complexes and alloys, some of which have been demonstrated as superior OER electrocatalysts [12–19]. However, the low electrical conductivity of these oxides has limited their development for practical applications.

Along with continuing research on and synthesis of noble metal-based OER electrocatalysts [11–20], carbon-based nanohybrid materials have drawn significant attention as potential OER electrocatalysts owing to their superior electrical conductivity and unique electronic structure [21–23]. Additionally, the nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) surface containing nitrogen atoms (electron-donating) can significantly modify the electronic structures of the host, providing a promising way to tailor the electron-transport properties of graphene for OER application. Su et al. [24] synthesized N-GCNT/FeCo bifunctional electrocatalysts through solid-state reactions. A strong synergistic interaction between FeCo and N-doped carbon nanotubes offers a favorable local coordination environment, which offers N-rich electrocatalyst active sites to promote the reversible oxygen reaction. The N-GCNT/FeCo electrocatalysts exhibit an operating potential of 1.73 V at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slope of 99.5 mV dec−1. Wang et al. [25] synthesized Fe/Fe3C@C (Fe@C) nanoparticles encapsulated in 3D nitrogen-doped (N-doped) graphene and bamboo-like CNT and investigated their OER application in a 1 M KOH electrolyte solution. The Fe@C structure generates the additional electron on a carbon surface, promoting OER on adjacent Fe–N active sites. It exhibited 450 mV overpotentials at a current density of 10 mA cm−2 and high stability after 1,000 cycles. Gao et al. [26] fabricated a composite of nitrogen-doped graphene-supported CoSe2 for OER application. It had a low Tafel value of 40 mV dec−1 at a 10 mA cm−2 current density, which is quite close to that of RuO2. This improvement occurred due to the synergetic effect of N-doped graphene and CoSe2. Liu et al. [27] synthesized ZnCo2O4 quantum dots anchored on N-doped carbon nanotube electrocatalysts and investigated their OER application in a 0.1 M KOH electrolyte solution. The ZnCo2O4/N-CNT had a 430 mV overpotential at a current density of 10 mA cm−2, which is lower than that of ZnCo2O4 because N-CNT enhances the electrical conductivity and has a large surface area to interact with the electrolyte. Single-atom catalysts (SACs) could maximize the utility of metal atoms and therefore offer great potential for water splitting [28–32]. Zhang et al. reported an isolated Ni and N atoms-decorated carbon composite (HCM@Ni–N). The HCM@NiN shows that effective electronic coupling via Ni–N coordination is vital for regulating the performance of OER [33,34]. Despite many efforts by research groups, developing monodispersed Fe(NO)2 moieties anchored nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) to enhance electrocatalytic performance and deciphering the exact active regions for the OER are still limited. We report, for the first time, an N-GQDs dinitrosyl iron complex (Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs) OER electrocatalyst composed of homogeneously dispersed Fe(NO)2 fragments anchored on N sites of N-GQDs, which are synthesized using N-GQDs and dicarbonyl dinitrosyl iron (Fe(CO)2(NO)2) precursors. The advantage of the nitrosyl group is that NO can act as a non-innocent ligand to regulate the electronic property of the Fe(NO)2 core and to stabilize the iron during OER through easy back-and-forth electron transfer [35,36]. Moreover, the N-GQDs offer superior electrical conductivity to boost electron transportation to the moiety of Fe–N–C configuration. The 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex delivers excellent OER performance with a low potential of 1.59 V at 48 mA cm−2 and a Tafel slope of 48 mV dec−1, which are superior to those of the commercial RuO2 (1.69 V at 45 mA cm−2) electrocatalyst under alkaline conditions. Moreover, the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curve of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex collected after 2,000 cycles of testing is nearly identical to the first scan, without significant changes in the curve shapes. These results demonstrate a significant breakthrough in OER efficiency for a non-precious and monodispersed electrocatalyst system.

Graphite powder, iron pentacarbonyl (Fe(CO)5), sodium nitrite (Na(NO)2), nitrosonium tetrafluoroborate (NOBF4), Nafion, polyethyleneimine (PEI), and 15-crown-5 (C10H20O5) purchased from Sigma-Aldrich were used without any further purification. Potassium hydroxide (KOH) and pyridinium tribromide (PyBr3) were purchased from Alfa Aesar and Tokyo Chemical Industrial Co. Ltd., respectively. Ethanol (C2H5OH) and tetrahydrofuran (THF) solvents were purchased from ECHO chemical Co., Ltd. Magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) was bought from the Nippon Shinyaku Co., Ltd. All chemicals were used as received for the experiment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs)
ce:section.ce:para: N-GQDs (equal to 0-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs) were synthesized using the hydrothermal method previously reported [37]. Briefly, 25 mg of graphite powder was mixed in 100 mL of 1 M PyBr3 aqueous solution (1 M PyBr3 is prepared in the 1:1 vol ratio of double distilled water and ethanol) and this mixture was sonicated for 2 h to obtain a pyridinium-assisted exfoliated graphene (py-graphene) sheets suspension. The py-graphene sheets suspension (50 mL) was mixed with 10 g PEI and this mixture was transferred to a stainless autoclave reactor at 230 °C for 8 h and cooled. The resulting solution was filtered using a 0.22 μm PTFE syringe filter and then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm for 30 min to collect the high-concentration N-GQDs solution.

Dicarbonyl dinitrosyl iron (Fe(CO)2(NO)2) was synthesized using the reported method [38,39]. Briefly, mix NaNO2 with 15-crown-5 and Fe(CO)5 in a molar ratio of 1:1:1 in the THF solvent. When 15-crown-5 combines with Na+ of NaNO2 to form a complex, the oxygen atom of the NO2 − moiety combines with the CO of the Fe(CO)5 to form CO2 gas, and then the remaining NO is to replace the CO position of the Fe(CO)5, which produces [Fe(CO)3NO]−. Then, the [Fe(CO)3NO]− was mixed with an excess of NOBF4 to carry out the nitrosation reaction and wait for the reaction to complete in an ice bath for 1 h to obtain Fe(CO)2(NO)2.

Then, 0.0085 mg of Fe(CO)2(NO)2 was transferred into a round-bottom flask with 3 mL of N-GQDs and a small amount of MgSO4. This mixture was kept in a liquid nitrogen ice bath and attached to the fractionation device to remove the unwanted side product or gases formed and/or generated during the reaction. This system was left for a day to complete the reaction and obtain the pure compound, named 0.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. The 1.0-Fe(NO)2–N-GQD, 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQD, and 2.0-Fe(NO)2–N-GQD complexes were also prepared by the same synthesis procedure, which is used above to prepare Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, except for that only the weight of Fe(CO)2(NO)2 is changed to 0.0170 mg, 0.0255 mg, and 0.0340 mg, respectively. The color of the unfinished material is bright orange and that of the completed reaction is brown. The loading amount of the Fe component in the 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs hybrid is 2.62 ppm, which is measured by ICP-MS.

All synthesized materials were characterized for the composition, structural, morphological, and electrochemical oxygen evolution properties. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were performed on a PHI Model 590 spectrometer with multiprobes (Al Kα radiation, λ = 1,486.6 eV) for oxidation state and material composition confirmation. The structure and phase of the material were confirmed by Raman spectroscopy (using a 532 nm laser source with an iHR550 spectrometer; HORIBA Jobin Yvon). Time-resolved photoluminescence spectroscopy was carried out using a time-correlated single-photon spectrometer (Picoquant, Inc.). A 375 nm pulse laser was used for excitation. X-ray diffraction measurements were obtained using a Bruker D8 Discover SSS Multifunction high powder X-ray diffractometer. Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a PerkinElmer Frontier spectrometer to investigate the characteristic functional groups of the samples. The specific surface areas were obtained using the Brunauer–Emmett–Teller (ASAP2060; Micromeritics) method. Dynamic light scattering (DLS; Zetasizer Nano S90, Malvern Panalytical Ltd.) was used to measure the particle distribution. The loading amount of Fe was characterized using an Agilent ICP-MS 7700e. The morphologies were characterized using a JEOL JSM-7600F field-emission scanning electron microscope and the sizes of the materials were investigated using JEOL Hitachi H-7100 and JEOL JEM-ARM200FTH high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) images. The electrochemical performance was studied using an electrochemical workstation (CHI 7927E).

The electrochemical OER measurement of an Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs sample was carried out in the three-electrode setup, with glassy carbon, graphite, and Hg/HgCl2 as the working, counter, and reference electrode, respectively. The working electrode was prepared as follows: 2.6 mg of Fe(NO)2–N-GQD samples were mixed in 0.1 mL of 5 wt % Nafion solution. Then, the mixture was stirred for 10 min to form a homogeneous ink. Afterward, 10 μL of this mixture was dropped on the surface of a rotating ring disk electrode (RRDE) and placed in an oven at 70 °C for 30 min to dry the loaded mixture, which gave the final OER electrocatalyst loading of 0.26 mg cm−2 on the working electrode. The Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs loaded working electrode was then mounted on the RRDE device with a fixed rotation speed of 1,600 rpm under OER measurements. The RuO2 catalytic working electrode was prepared with the method used by Wu et al. [40] The whole OER electrochemical performance, such as LSV and stability performed in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte, and all electrochemical results were further reported with respect to RHE in 0.1 M KOH. The electrocatalyst stability testing was carried out by chronoamperometric measurement at a constant overpotential of −380 mV with respect to RHE. Continuous cyclic voltammetry (CV) scans were obtained in a potential window from 1 to 1.8 V vs. RHE (without iR-compensated values) at the scan rate of 10 mV s−1. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) performances were carried out from 0.1 Hz to 100 kHz with an amplitude of 5 mV at the potential (1.55 V) of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. The iR correction of LSV curves was performed using the electrolyte resistance (Rs) obtained from EIS measurements. The potential was calculated using the equation: E(iR-corrected) = E − iR, where i is the current and R is the electrolyte resistance. All electrochemical results were further reported with respect to an RHE in 0.1 M KOH: E(RHE) = E(Hg/HgCl2) + 0.130 + 0.0592 × pH.

Fig. 1 shows the schematic synthetic procedure for Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. Briefly, the graphite powder was exfoliated by PyBr3 to obtain a graphene suspension, which is confirmed by TEM analysis (Fig. S1). Then, the graphene suspension was used in the hydrothermal method to synthesize N-GQDs (Fig. S2). The as-synthesized N-GQDs and the precursor Fe(CO)2(NO)2 were mixed to form the OER electrocatalyst, Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. To identify the formation of coordination bonding within the Fe and N-GQDs, FT-IR measurements were carried out. Fig. 2 a shows the FT-IR spectra of Fe(CO)2(NO)2, Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, and PyBr3Fe(NO)2, which are indicated by the black, red, and green line, respectively. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe(CO)2(NO)2 found four absorption bands. The absorption bands at 1,806 and 1,759 cm−1 correspond to N–O signals, while the absorption bands at 2,087 and 2,037 cm−1 correspond to C–O [41,42]. These results are well-matched with literature reports and confirm the successful formation of the Fe(CO)2(NO)2 complex [43]. The FT-IR spectrum of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs only showed the two absorption bands at 1,773 and 1,707 cm−1, which correspond to the N–O stretching vibrations [37–44]. Compared to the FT-IR spectrum of Fe(CO)2(NO)2, the absorption band of the C–O group (stretching vibration at 2,087 and 2,037 cm−1) completely disappears in the FT-IR spectrum of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, which confirms that the nitrogen atoms in the N-GQDs replaced all the C–O functional groups and formed the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex. Moreover, the N–O stretching bands are slightly shifted toward the lower wavenumber region compared to the FT-IR spectrum of Fe(CO)2(NO)2, indicating the role of N-GQDs in metal coordination, which supports the formation of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex. However, the nitrogen sources of the unreacted chemicals, PyBr3 and PEI, could form either pyridinic-Fe or –NH-Fe configurations. To clearly verify these two possibilities, FT-IR is a powerful tool to identify the formation of PyBr3-Fe(NO)2 and PEI-Fe(NO)2 complexes. Fig. 2a shows that specific peaks of C–O and N–O are not found in the complexes of PyBr3-Fe(CO)2(NO)2 and PEI-Fe(CO)2(NO)2, indicating that the environmental-reaction conditions did not favor these complexes. This result confirmed that only N-GQDs containing nitrogen can replace the CO ligand of the Fe(CO)2(NO)2 to form the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex.

Fig. 2b shows the Raman spectra of graphene, N-GQDs, and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. All three materials reveal two bands at ~1,358 cm−1 and ~1,590 cm−1, which correspond to the defect band (D band) and graphene band (G band), respectively [45]. The D band corresponds to the structure defects (sp3 carbon atoms) and edges of graphene flakes, while the G band corresponds to the sp2-hybridized carbon atoms. The ID/IG ratio was calculated for all materials and is 0.74 (graphene), 0.87 (N-GQDs), and 0.87 (Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs). From the above ID/IG ratio measurements, Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs show an identical ID/IG value to N-GQDs, which indicates that the functionalization of Fe(NO)2 moieties maintains the original surface structure of N-GQDs. The XRD patterns of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complexes and N-GQDs are shown in Fig. S3. The XRD pattern of the N-GQDs has a broad peak, which indicates the thinness and disordered structure of the N-GQDs [46–48]. The XRD peaks of Fe(NO)2–N-GQD complexes are nearly identical to the results of the N-GQDs, indicating that no diffraction peaks of metal Fe or Fe oxide compounds were detected in the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs samples. This is attributed to the monodispersed Fe(NO)2 functionalization on the N-GQDs surface. The UV–visible spectra were recorded for N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs and the results are shown in Fig. 2c. The UV–visible spectrum of N-GQDs detected two significant absorption peaks at 256 nm and ~362 nm; these are characteristic peaks of π → π∗ for the CC transition of sp2 and the n → π∗ transition of sp3, respectively. This result shows the successful doping of N atoms on GQDs [37–49]. After Fe(NO)2 moiety addition in N-GQDs to form Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, the UV–visible peak underwent a blue shift from 362 nm to 340 nm, which is attributed to the binding of nitrogen-based functional groups of N-GQDs with metal [50–53]. Fig. S4 shows the emission spectra with an excitation wavelength (λex) at 450 nm, revealing the difference between the N-GQDs and the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex. A blue-shift in the fluorescence emission wavelength (λem) of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs occurred, demonstrating that the Fe(NO)2 ligands were successfully anchored onto the N-GQDs.

The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) image shows that the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs (Fig. 3 a) had a uniform size distribution around 3.0 nm. Elemental mapping analysis depicts that the monodispersed Fe(NO)2 (Fig. 3b) are homogeneously distributed throughout the N-GQDs. Fig. 3c shows that the atomic percent of Fe is 0.01% as shown in Table S1. Moreover, the HRTEM image and FFT image in Fig. S5 show the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs with a uniform and hexagonal structure, indicating that the graphene structure of the N-GQDs maintains its original structure. The HAADF-STEM-EDS mappings (Fig. 4 b and c) show distribution of Fe and N in the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex, indicating Fe(NO)2 coordinated on N sites of the N-GQDs.

To precisely determine the chemical composition of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, XPS was performed. Fig. 5 a shows that the C 1s spectrum of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs can be decoupled into five peaks that correspond to CC (284.6 eV), defective C–N (285.0 eV), CN (285.9 eV), C–O (286.5 eV), and CO (287.5 eV) functional groups, indicating both graphene structure and N-derivatives on the surface of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. Fig. 5b shows that the N 1s spectrum can be decoupled into five peaks that correspond to pyridinic N (398.2 eV), N–Fe compounds (399.1 eV), benzenoid amine (–NH–) (399.6 eV), graphitic N (400.8 eV), and oxidized nitrogen compounds (401.9 eV) [54–58]. Among the five, the N 1s core-level spectrum with a binding energy of about 399.1 eV confirmed that Fe was successfully coordinated to the N-GQDs [59]. The Fe 2p spectrum (Fig. 5c) can also be decoupled into five peaks at 710.6 eV (Fe2+ 2p3/2), 712.3 eV (Fe3+ 2p3/2), 719.0 eV (satellite peak for the above four peaks), 723.2 eV (Fe2+ 2p1/2), and 725.6 eV (Fe3+ 2p1/2) [60]. The mixed valence compound of Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs is due to the non-innocent ligand of NO that can become oxidized (NO+), radical (NO.), or reduced (NO−) when binding to a transition metal [61].

The OER electrocatalytic activity of the as-prepared samples was investigated through electrochemical measurements using the three-electrode system in 0.1 M KOH electrolyte at a scan rate of 5 mV s−1. Fig. S6 shows the OER performance of graphene, indicating that non-functionalized graphene shows inferior OER performance. Fig. 6 a shows the comparative LSV curves of N-GQDs, Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, and RuO2. The N-GQDs exhibit poor potential compared with the RuO2. However, the 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex demonstrates a lower onset potential (defined as the onset potential at 1 mA cm−2) [62–64] value of 1.46 V for the OER activity, which greatly outperforms that of the state-of-the-art RuO2 (1.51 V). Moreover, at a potential of 1.59 V, the 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs exhibit a current density of 48 mA cm−2, while the current density of RuO2 is 10 mA cm−2. These results mean that the 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQD electrocatalysts can generate higher amounts of oxygen compared to those of the RuO2. The Tafel slopes of the N-GQDs, Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs, and RuO2 are shown in Fig. 5b. The 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs show a Tafel slope of 48 mV dec−1, which is lower than the 82 mV dec−1 for the RuO2, implying favorable OER kinetics for a monodispersed Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs electrocatalyst. Compared with previous reported carbon-related catalysts (Table S2), the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs catalyst exhibited a downshift in the onset potential, indicating a superior OER performance.

To understand the mechanism of the introduction of Fe(NO)2 for improving OER performance, FeCl2 is mixed with the N-GQDs to form the possible configuration of FeCl2/N-GQDs as a control experiment. As shown in Fig. S7, FeCl2/N-GQDs had better OER performance than the N-GQDs. This means that the anchored Fe of the FeCl2/N-GQDs can facilitate the OER reaction. However, the OER performance of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs exhibited further OER enhancement than FeCl2/N-GQDs. EIS was then used to analyze the kinetics of charge-transfer involved in the OER. The EIS curves of Fig. 6c were fitted by the 2-time constant series (2TS) model (Fig. S8). Fig. 6c shows that the fitted semicircular diameter of the 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs (Rct = 35 Ω) is smaller than that of the N-GQDs (Rct = 107 Ω), indicating that the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs had a lower charge transfer resistance than the N-GQDs, implying that the monatomic dispersed Fe(NO)2 was anchored on the N-GQDs to enhance electrical conductivity. These results support the Tafel plot results. We further probed the interaction between N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs by measuring the emission decay using a 375 nm pulsed laser as the excitation source. The lifetime recorded at the emission maximum showed a multiexponential decay and was fitted using bi-exponential decay kinetics. The curves were fitted well with a biexponential decay function and the results are shown in Fig. 6d. The average value of τem (decay lifetime) at a given λex (excitation wavelength) was calculated using FluoFit software. These values are consolidated in Table 1 . The average fluorescence lifetime value of N-GQDs is dropped from 8.6 ns to 1.86 ns when N-GQDs were anchored with the Fe(NO)2 moiety. The observed decrease in the emission lifetime is because the Fe(NO)2 moiety interaction with N-GQDs results in charge transfer quenching. Therefore, the rates of the charge separation sequence are in the reverse order of their emission lifetimes [65]. The Fe(NO)2 moiety is indeed coordinated with N-GQDs. Therefore, the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs show superior OER performance.

To gain deep insight into the enhanced OER activity, the electrochemically active surface area (ECSA) was measured by the double-layer capacitance of the N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs. The capacitive current density as a function of the scan rate was used as a useful indicator of the ECSA. Fig. 7 a–e show the CV profiles of Fe(NO)2–N-GQD electrodes of different ratios. Fig. 7f shows that the 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs sample showed the steepest slope, indicating that it refers to the largest active surface area toward OER. The enhancement in ECSA for Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs suggests that more reaction sites are accessible for OER upon using Fe(NO)2 functionalization. In addition, the long-term stability of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs electrode was evaluated. Chronoamperometric measurement demonstrated that the Fe(NO)2–N-GQD electrodes were stable at the current density of 50 mA cm−2 for more than 4 h (Fig. 8 a), affirming the superior stability of an Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs electrocatalyst. Another critical criterion for a good electrocatalyst is durability. To assess the durability of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs electrocatalyst, continuous CV was performed in the 1–1.8 V voltage range with an RHE for over 2,000 cycles (Fig. 8b). Very little change was observed in the LSV curve of the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs electrocatalyst after scanning 2,000 cycles, indicating that the Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs electrocatalyst was a durable OER electrocatalyst. The Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs showed excellent OER activity because of the synergetic effect of N-GQDs and Fe(NO)2 moieties. The N-GQDs have a large surface area, good electronic conductivity, and doped nitrogen atoms enhancing the electron density. When N-GQDs form the complex with the Fe(NO)2 moiety, the surface structure or electronic properties of active sites might change, and these are the most impactful parameters for OER activity [66,67].

In this work, a novel Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex was synthesized by mixing N-GQDs and Fe(CO)2(NO)2, by the chemical route, and used as an electrocatalyst. The 1.5-Fe(NO)2–N-GQDs complex exhibits an OER onset potential of 1.46 V and a Tafel slope of 48 mV dec−1 in 0.1 M KOH. Moreover, it shows a higher current density above 48 mA cm−2 (@1.59 V) while the RuO2 has a current density of only up to 45 mA cm−2 (@1.69 V); this improvement appeared due to the synergetic effects of N-GQDs (provide large surface area) and Fe(NO)2 moieties. Therefore, this Fe(NO)2 coordination strategy we developed will inspire many opportunities to develop various novel SACs as highly active and stable electrocatalysts for OER and other reactions.

C.-Y. Chang: Methodology, Data curation. A.A. Kashale: Investigation, Data curation. C.-M. Lee: Data curation. S.-L. Chu: Data curation. Y.-F. Lin: Data curation. I-W.P. Chen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Writing – original draft.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to thank Chia-Ying Chien, Su-Jen Ji, and Yin-Mei Chang for their assistance with SEM, EDS, XPS, and TEM experiments at NTU and NTHU Precious Instrument Center. This research is supported by MOST (109-2628-M-143-001-MY3), Taiwan.
